skill.name: datacommons-client
skill.description: Use Data Commons Python API guidance to resolve places, query public statistical observations, explore graph relationships, and prepare analysis-ready results.
skill.summary: Query and analyze public statistics with the Data Commons Python client.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many command execution and weak cryptography patterns, but review shows they are Markdown code fences, inline API examples, and Data Commons identifiers rather than executable skill code. Real risks are limited to documented outbound calls to Data Commons, API key setup guidance, and one example that writes a local CSV file.

low_findings.title: Documented API Key Handling
low_findings.description: The skill instructs users to configure a Data Commons API key through DC_API_KEY or a constructor argument. This is legitimate authentication guidance, but users should avoid pasting real secrets into prompts or shared logs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly document API key setup for Data Commons. The usage appears legitimate and no exfiltration destination or hidden credential collection was found.

low_findings.title: Expected Outbound Data Commons Requests
low_findings.description: Examples call DataCommonsClient methods that contact Data Commons services to resolve places and fetch public statistics. This network access matches the skill purpose and is not hidden behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is visible in documented API examples and aligns with the stated data-querying purpose. No evidence found of unrelated external endpoints or data exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Example Writes Analysis Output to CSV
low_findings.description: One getting started example exports a comparison table to ca_cities_comparison.csv. This is an ordinary analysis output, but users should review file paths before running adapted examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem write is explicit and limited to a CSV export example. It is not hidden or destructive, but it is still a real local file write pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Command Execution Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
dangerous_patterns.description: The reported Ruby and shell backtick execution findings correspond to Markdown code fences and inline code markers in documentation. Review found no executable skill script that runs these snippets automatically.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens are Markdown syntax and Python documentation examples, not Ruby backtick execution. No shell command construction or automatic execution path was found in the reviewed files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
dangerous_patterns.description: The weak cryptography alerts appear to match ordinary words, Markdown, or Data Commons identifier text rather than cryptographic API usage. No hashing, cipher, or password storage logic was found.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the reported areas shows documentation prose and API examples only. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic operations.

content.user_title: Query Public Statistics with Data Commons
content.value_statement: Public statistics often require entity resolution, variable discovery, and careful source selection. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Data Commons workflows for reliable analysis.
actual_capabilities: Explains Data Commons Python client installation with and without Pandas support.
Shows how to resolve place names, coordinates, and Wikidata IDs into Data Commons IDs.
Guides observation queries for population, income, unemployment, health, and environmental variables.
Documents node endpoint patterns for properties, names, hierarchies, and graph relationships.
Demonstrates conversion of observation responses into dictionaries and Pandas DataFrames.
Describes source filtering with facets for more consistent statistical comparisons.
limitations: Requires access to the Data Commons Python client and network access to Data Commons services.
The default datacommons.org service requires a Data Commons API key.
Resolve endpoint guidance focuses on place entities, not every knowledge graph entity type.
Statistical availability depends on Data Commons coverage, source freshness, and selected variables.

use_cases.title: Compare Regional Indicators
use_cases.description: Query population, income, unemployment, or other indicators across states, counties, cities, or countries.

use_cases.title: Prepare Public Data Features
use_cases.description: Resolve place names and fetch standardized statistics for dashboards, notebooks, or model inputs.

use_cases.title: Explore Geographic Relationships
use_cases.description: Use the knowledge graph to discover place hierarchies, entity names, properties, and related nodes.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Query
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner user needs a first Data Commons request.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the datacommons-client skill to show how to query the latest population for California and Texas.

prompt_templates.title: Resolve Places First
prompt_templates.scenario: User has place names but not Data Commons IDs.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the datacommons-client skill to resolve these places to DCIDs, handle ambiguous matches, and then query Count_Person: [places].

prompt_templates.title: Build a Time Series Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the datacommons-client skill to fetch all available unemployment observations for the United States and convert the response into a Pandas-friendly table.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Multi-Source Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user needs consistent comparisons across entities.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the datacommons-client skill to design a workflow that compares county-level median household income, checks variable availability, filters by source facets, and documents limitations.

output_examples.input: Find current population for California, Texas, and New York.
output_examples.output: A concise workflow that resolves each state name, fetches Count_Person for the latest date, and presents a comparison table with entity names and dates.

output_examples.input: Analyze unemployment trends for the United States.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step plan for requesting all dates, converting observations into records, sorting by date, and plotting or summarizing the trend.

output_examples.input: Explore what data exists for San Francisco.
output_examples.output: Guidance to resolve the city, fetch available statistical variables, retrieve entity metadata, and select relevant indicators before querying observations.

best_practices: Resolve names to DCIDs before querying observations or graph relationships.
Check variable availability before building comparisons across many places.
Filter by facet domains when consistent sources matter for analysis.
anti_patterns: Do not assume a place name maps to one unique Data Commons entity.
Do not paste real API keys into prompts, examples, notebooks, or shared logs.
Do not compare statistics from mixed sources without reviewing facets and provenance.

faq.question: What is Data Commons?
faq.answer: Data Commons is a public knowledge graph and API for statistical data from many authoritative sources.

faq.question: Does this skill run Data Commons queries automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides instructions and examples for using the Data Commons Python client in your environment.

faq.question: Do I need an API key?
faq.answer: The default datacommons.org service requires a Data Commons API key. Custom Data Commons instances may not require one.

faq.question: Can it work with Pandas?
faq.answer: Yes. The references show how to install Pandas support and convert observations into analysis-ready records.

faq.question: What entities can the resolve endpoint handle?
faq.answer: The skill documents place resolution by name, coordinates, and Wikidata ID. Other entity types may require node exploration.

faq.question: How should I handle ambiguous place names?
faq.answer: Review all candidates, use entity type filters, or provide more specific names before selecting a DCID.
